revision.comment: Bot: remove [[Category:Shinto shrine]] (crud category cleanup) [[github:EmmaLeonhart/shintowiki-scripts/actions/runs/24699703697|time triggered pipeline]]
revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:二上山 (鳥取県)]] <!--generated by generate_shikinaisha_pages_v25_with_redirects.py--> {{Expand Japanese|二上山 (鳥取県)}}
{{Infobox religious building | name = Futagami-yama Shrine (Tottori Prefecture) | native_name = 二上山 | mapframe = yes | mapframe-zoom = 15 | mapframe-wikidata = yes | mapframe-point = none | map_type = Japan | map_caption = Futagami-yama Shrine (Tottori Prefecture) | coordinates = {{coord|35.558161|134.369163}} | map_relief = 1 | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] }}
{{nihongo|'''Futagami-yama Shrine (Tottori Prefecture)'''|二上山}} is a [[shinto shrine]] in the [[Engishiki Jinmyōchō]]. It is located in [[Inaba Province]].
== instance of (P31) ==
* {{ill|Mountain|lt=mountain|ace|Gunong|af|Berg|als|Berg|am|ተራራ|ang|Beorg|ann|Ogoon̄|an|Montanya|arc|ܛܘܪܐ|ar|جبل|arnews|تصنيف:جبال|arquote|جبل|arz|جبل|ast|Monte|as|পৰ্বত|awa|पहाड़|azb|داغ|az|Dağ|azquote|Dağ|ban|Gunung|bar|Beag|bat_smg|Kalns|ba|Тау|bcl|Bukid|bdr|Belud|be_x_old|Гара|be|Гара|bew|Gunung|bg|Планина|bh|पहाड़|bjn|Gunung|blk|ကောင်|bn|পর্বত|bpy|মোন্টানহা|br|Menez|bs|Planina|ca|Muntanya|caquote|Muntanya|cdo|Săng|ceb|Bukid|ce|Лам|chr|ᎣᏓᎸᎢ|ckb|کێو|co|Muntagna|crh|Dağ|cs|Hora|csquote|Hora|cv|Ту|cy|Mynydd|dag|Zoli|da|Bjerg|de|Berg|dequote|Berg|dga|Taŋa|diq|Ko|el|Βουνό|eml|Muntâgna|en|Mountain|enquote|Mountain|eo|Monto|eoquote|Monto|es|Montaña|esquote|Montaña|et|Mägi|etquote|Mägi|eu|Mendi|fa|کوه|fiu_vro|Mägi|fi|Vuori|fiquote|Vuori|fj|Ulunivanua|fo|Fjall|frp|Montagne|frr|Berag|fr|Montagne|frquote|Montagne|fy|Berch|gan|山|ga|Sliabh|gcr|Montangn|gd|Beinn|gl|Montaña|gn|Yvyty|gom|Dongor|gor|Hu'idu|gu|પર્વત|gv|Slieau|hak|Sân|ha|Tsauni|haw|Mauna|he|הר|hequote|הר|hif|Pahaarr|hi|पर्वत|hiquote|पर्वत|hr|Planina|hsb|Hora|ht|Mòn|hu|Hegy|hy|Լեռ|hyw|Սար|ia|Montania|id|Gunung|ii|ꁧ|ik|Iġġi|ilo|Bantay|inh|Лоам|io|Monto|is|Fjall|it|Montagna|itquote|Montagna|jam|Mountn|ja|山|jaquote|山|jbo|cmana|jv|Gunung|kaa|Taw|kab|Adrar == elevation above sea level (P2044) ==
* {'amount': '+330', 'unit': 'http://www.wikidata.org/entity/Q11573'}
== VIAF cluster ID (P214) ==
* 315126575
== street address (P6375) ==
* {'text': '鳥取県岩美郡岩美町岩常213', 'language': 'ja'}
[[JA:二上山 (鳥取県)]]
{{wikidata link|Q21654507}}
== Japanese content == {{Infobox mountain |名称 = Mount Futagami |画像 = ファイル:二上山（鳥取県岩美町）.jpg |画像キャプション = Seen from the Iwatsune district on the northeastern foot |標高 = 330 |座標 = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|35|32|25.3|N|134|19|8.2|E|type:mountain_region:JP-31|display=inline,title}} |所在地 = {{JPN}}{{ill|lt=Tottori Prefecture|Q133935|qid=Q133935|1=Tottori Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Iwami District, Tottori|Q1131619|qid=Q1131619|1=Iwami District, Tottori}}{{ill|lt=Iwami, Tottori|Q281925|qid=Q281925|1=Iwami, Tottori}} |山系 = |種類 = |初登頂 = |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Mapplot|coordinates={{Coord|35.540376|134.318955}}|caption=|width=300}}<small>Location of Mount Futagami</small>{{日本の位置情報|35|32|25.3|134|19|8.2|二上山|35.540376,134.318955|二上山}} }}__NOTOC__
'''Mount Futagami''' (ふたがみやま<ref name="百名山_二上山"/>、ふたかみやま<ref name="東伍_二上山"/>) is a mountain in {{ill|lt=Iwami, Tottori|Q281925|qid=Q281925|1=Iwami, Tottori}}, {{ill|lt=Iwami District, Tottori|Q1131619|qid=Q1131619|1=Iwami District, Tottori}}, {{ill|lt=Tottori Prefecture|Q133935|qid=Q133935|1=Tottori Prefecture}}. It has an elevation of 330 m{{refnest|group="注"|There is no triangulation point on the summit; on GSI maps there is only a 333 m spot elevation near the summit based on {{ill|lt=photogrammetry|Q190149|qid=Q190149|1=Photogrammetry}}. The 'Nihon Chimei Daijiten 31: Tottori Prefecture ({{ill|lt=Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten|Q4093011|qid=Q4093011|1=Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten}})' gives “334 m<ref name="角川_二上山"/>,” the 'Shin Bunken Tozan Guide 30: Mountains of Tottori Prefecture' gives “330 m<ref name="分県_二上山"/>,” and the 'Shin Nihon Sangakushi' gives “about 330 m<ref name="新日本_二上山"/>,” etc.}}. It is one of the {{ill|lt=100 Famous Mountains of Chūgoku|Q11363405|qid=Q11363405|1=100 Famous Chūgoku Mountains}}.
It is said that there was once a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|qid=Q134917286}} called “Futagami Shrine.” In the medieval period, {{ill|lt=Futagamiyama Castle|Q11371272|qid=Q11371272|1=Futagamiyama Castle}} was built here, and it was the base of the {{ill|lt=Yamana clan|Q2975406|qid=Q2975406|1=Yamana clan}}, {{ill|lt=shugo|Q1141023|qid=Q1141023|1=Shugo}} of {{ill|lt=Inaba Province|Q1054338|qid=Q1054338|1=Inaba Province}}<ref name="角川_二上山"/><ref name="新日本_二上山"/>.
Near the summit is a flat area called “Ichi-no-naru (Ichi-no-taira),” created because the summit was cut down when the castle was built. Stone walls surrounding Ichi-no-taira remain, and castle remains such as {{ill|lt=kuruwa|Q6447542|qid=Q6447542|1=Kuruwa}}, “Dan,” and “Ni-no-taira” are scattered across the mountain. As “Futagamiyama Castle Ruins,” the site is designated by Tottori Prefecture as a {{ill|lt=historic site|Q1081138|qid=Q1081138|1=Historic site}}<ref name="分県_二上山"/><ref name="新日本_二上山"/><ref name="TW_岩美_文化財"/>.
More than 30 kofun have been found from the foot of the mountain; among them, “Takanozaka Kofun” was relocated for preservation and made into a park. A trail has been developed from the regional farm road running along the mid-slope, and the summit can be reached in just under one hour on the outbound climb<ref name="分県_二上山"/><ref name="百名山_二上山"/>.
=== Mountain name === The present mountain name is said to derive from {{ill|lt=Yamana Tokiuji|Q11467593|qid=Q11467593|1=Yamana Tokiuji}}, who in the {{ill|lt=Nanboku-chō period|Q264012|qid=Q264012|lt=Northern and Southern Courts period|1=Nanboku-chō period}} during the {{ill|lt=Bunna|Q1146252|qid=Q1146252|lt=Bunna era|1=Bunna}} ({{ill|lt=1352|Q6112|qid=Q6112|1=1352}}–{{ill|lt=1355|Q6123|qid=Q6123|1=1355}}) built a castle on the summit and named it “'''{{ill|lt=Futagamiyama Castle|Q11371272|qid=Q11371272|1=Futagamiyama Castle}}'''”; from then on the mountain came to be called “Futagami-yama”<ref name="角川_二上山"/><ref name="新日本_二上山"/>.
There are various theories regarding what it was called before that.
* The name “Futagami-yama” is generally used for mountains with two peaks, but this mountain is not of that shape. {{ill|lt=Tateiwa-yama (Tottori Prefecture)|UNKNOWN|lt=Tateiwa-yama}}, northwest of this mountain, has two peaks, and “Futagami-yama” originally referred to what is now Tateiwa-yama. When the Yamana clan built their castle, they took the name from nearby “Futagami-yama,” and over time this mountain came to be called “Futagami-yama”<ref name="角川_二上山"/>. Another theory interprets the name “Futagami-yama” as “two mountains called as a pair,” meaning a collective name for present-day Tateiwa-yama and Futagami-yama<ref name="新日本_二上山"/>.
* In some historical materials it is written as “Nikami-yama (二神山).” There is a theory that the original name was “Nikamiyama,” and the Edo-period '{{ill|lt=Inabashi|Q11420257|qid=Q11420257|1=Inabashi}}' adopts this view<ref name="百名山_二上山"/>.
=== Takanozaka Kofun Cluster === [[ファイル:高野坂古墳群（鳥取県岩美町）の横穴式石室.jpg|right|thumb|No. 10 mound with a horizontal stone chamber (relocated and reconstructed)]] Over 30 archaeological remains have been found on the slopes of Mount Futagami, and they are called the '''Takanozaka Kofun Cluster''' (たかんざかこふんぐん) {{refnest|group="注"|The area on the eastern foot of Mount Futagami is presumed to have been called “Takano-gō” in antiquity. “Takano-gō” appears in the Heian-period '{{ill|lt=Wamyō Ruijushō|Q2702542|qid=Q2702542|1=Wamyō Ruijushō}},' but no reading is specified, so it is uncertain. Because a “Takano Shrine” identified with the Takano Shrine (Takano-no-jinja) in the Engishiki Jinmyōchō is in this area, and because this area continued to be called Takano-gō after the medieval period, it is presumed that this was the Takano-gō in the Wamyō Ruijushō<ref name="平凡_高野郷"/>.}}. These were discovered during construction of a regional farm road, and are estimated to date from the end of the 5th century to the beginning of the 8th century. A four-year investigation began in 1987 (Shōwa 62), and many grave goods were excavated from six kofun with {{ill|lt=horizontal stone chambers|Q11543435|qid=Q11543435|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber}} and others<ref name="平凡_高野坂"/><ref name="分県_二上山"/>.
Among them, “No. 8 mound” on a ridge at elevation 94 m is a {{ill|lt=round tumulus|Q11394747|qid=Q11394747|1=Round Kofun}} about 13 m in diameter, estimated to date from the latter half of the 6th century. Its form matches kofun found in eastern Tottori Prefecture, but features such as the “triangular corner carrying-out technique” indicate it is among the earliest examples of this style<ref name="平凡_高野坂"/>.
The square “No. 10 mound” ({{ill|lt=hōfun|Q11504353|qid=Q11504353|1=Square Kofun}}) consists of a first tier of 12 m square and a second tier of 8 m square. The “'''house-shaped stone coffin'''” from this horizontal chamber is designated a protected cultural property (tangible cultural property) of Iwami Town<ref>{{PDFlink|[http://www.iwami.gr.jp/secure/5145/shiteibunkazai.pdf List of designated cultural properties]}} (Iwami Town website).</ref>. Eleven coffins of this type have been found in Tottori Prefecture, six of them in Iwami Town, and three of those are from the Takanozaka kofun. The house-shaped stone coffin in “No. 2 mound” uses {{ill|lt=Tatsuyama stone quarry remains#Tatsuyama stone|UNKNOWN|lt=Tatsuyama stone}}, a specialty of {{ill|lt=Harima Province|Q1047523|qid=Q1047523|1=Harima Province}}. These are generally estimated to date to the 7th century<ref name="平凡_高野坂"/><ref name="TW_岩美_文化財"/>.
Also, from No. 10 mound, a bronze {{ill|lt=abumi|Q191657|qid=Q191657|lt=tsubo-abumi (stirrup)|1=Stirrup}} grave good estimated to date from the latter half of the 7th century was discovered and designated a protected cultural property of Iwami Town. This stirrup has Buddhist [[:en:Palmette|honeysuckle-pattern]] cast-bronze decoration and was kept in a lacquered wooden box. No. 10 mound was relocated and still exists, developed as Takanozaka Kofun Park along the regional farm road<ref name="平凡_高野坂"/><ref name="分県_二上山"/><ref name="TW_岩美_文化財"/>.
=== Futagami Shrine === '''Futagami Shrine''' (ふたかみじんじゃ) is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|qid=Q134917286}} listed in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|qid=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}. Today, there is a “Futagami Shrine” in the Iwatsune district on the eastern foot, and this is identified with the Futagami Shrine in the Jinmyōchō<ref name="町誌_二上山"/><ref name="百名山_二上山"/>.
According to the Edo-period '{{ill|lt=Inabashi|Q11420257|qid=Q11420257|1=Inabashi}},' several shrines once stood on Mount Futagami, but when the Yamana clan built Futagamiyama Castle, they relocated all of them to “Tsuzumi-yama”<ref name="百名山_二上山"/><ref name="角川_二上山"/>.
Those on Mount Futagami were “Tsuzumi no Myōjin” in the mountain, “Tenjin-sha” to the east, “Kane-tsuki Myōjin” to the south, and “Hachimangū” to the north. While acknowledging other theories, the '{{ill|lt=Inabashi|Q11420257|qid=Q11420257|1=Inabashi}}' states that the destination name “Tsuzumi-yama” derives from the relocation of “Tsuzumi no Myōjin,” and identifies “Tsuzumi no Myōjin” with the “Futagami Shrine” of the Engishiki Jinmyōchō<ref name="百名山_二上山"/>. There is also a theory pointing out a connection between the Shikinaisha Futagami Shrine and the Takanozaka kofun cluster<ref name="角川_二上山"/>.
Tsuzumi Daimyōjin was enshrined on the right bank of the {{ill|lt=Odagawa River|Q21652810|qid=Q21652810|lt=Odagawa|1=Gamo River}} in Iwatsune village at the mountain foot and was renamed “Futagami Shrine” in the Meiji period<ref name="平凡_岩常"/>. Today it enshrines {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|lt=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|qid=Q272993|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto}}, and a festival is held in April<ref name="町誌_二上山"/>.
=== Futagamiyama Castle === *For details, see {{ill|lt=Futagamiyama Castle|Q11371272|qid=Q11371272|1=Futagamiyama Castle}}.
The {{ill|lt=Yamana clan|Q2975406|qid=Q2975406|1=Yamana clan}} originated in {{ill|lt=Kōzuke Province|Q1054838|qid=Q1054838|1=Kōzuke Province}}, but in the time of {{ill|lt=Yamana Tokiuji|Q11467593|qid=Q11467593|1=Yamana Tokiuji}}, they followed {{ill|lt=Ashikaga Takauji|Q297107|qid=Q297107|1=Ashikaga Takauji}} and defeated the {{ill|lt=Nawa clan|Q11415320|qid=Q11415320|1=Nawa clan}} and {{ill|lt=Enya clan|Q11428604|qid=Q11428604|lt=Shioji (Enya) clan|1=En'ya Takasada}}, who were aligned with the {{ill|lt=Southern Court (Japan)|Q3001082|qid=Q3001082|lt=Southern Court|1=Southern Court}} and based in the San'in region, and were granted shugo offices in the San'in region. Tokiuji built {{ill|lt=Futagamiyama Castle|Q11371272|qid=Q11371272|1=Futagamiyama Castle}} during the {{ill|lt=Bunna|Q1146252|qid=Q1146252|lt=Bunna era|1=Bunna}} (1352–1355) and made it his base. Thereafter, the Yamana family used it as their residence and ruled the San'in region for more than 100 years. The exact date is unclear, but sometime between the 15th and 16th centuries, the main base appears to have moved to {{ill|lt=Fuse-Tenzinyama Castle|Q11480764|qid=Q11480764|1=Tenjinyama Castle (Inaba)}} in western Inaba, and Futagamiyama Castle declined. The last leader based at Futagamiyama Castle may have been {{ill|lt=Yamana Ujiie|Q11467598|qid=Q11467598|1=Yamana Ujiie}}, but differences between sources are large and the matter is unclear<ref name="百名山_二上山"/><ref name="角川_二上山"/><ref name="PREF-鳥_文化財_二上"/>.
One theory says that in the {{ill|lt=Tenbun|Q1192853|qid=Q1192853|lt=Tenbun era|1=Tenbun}} (1532–1555), {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Mikami Toyonori|1=UNKNOWN}}, younger brother of {{ill|lt=Yamana Suketoyo|Q11467611|qid=Q11467611|1=Yamana Suketoyo}}, revived the castle; later, finding the summit location inconvenient, he newly built {{ill|lt=Dōchiku Castle|Q11641791|qid=Q11641791|1=Dōchiku Castle}} and moved there. After that, Futagamiyama Castle was abandoned<ref name="百名山_二上山"/>.
There are differing views about the Sengoku-period situation. According to sources such as '{{ill|lt=Inaba Mindanki|Q11420262|qid=Q11420262|1=Inaba Mindanki}},' even during Toyotomi Hideyoshi’s Inaba invasion in the {{ill|lt=Tenshō|Q1145058|qid=Q1145058|lt=Tenshō era|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)}}, it was still active as “Iwa-kyō no shiro,” and the name appears in Hideyoshi’s letters. “Iwa-kyō no shiro” was captured in 1580 (Tenshō 8), and together with 20,000 koku in {{ill|lt=Iwai District|Q11049025|qid=Q11049025|1=Iwai district}} (Kono District), it was granted to {{ill|lt=Kakiya Mitsunari|Q11425862|qid=Q11425862|1=Mitsunari Kakiya}}. Because “Iwa-kyō no shiro” was inconvenient, Mitsunari moved his base to {{ill|lt=Kiriyama Castle|Q11537196|qid=Q11537196|1=Kiriyama Castle}}. However, after the {{ill|lt=Battle of Sekigahara|Q234188|qid=Q234188|1=Battle of Sekigahara}}, the Kakiya clan, which had sided with the Western Army, was dispossessed, and the castle is said to have been abandoned at that time<ref name="平凡_二上山城"/>.
Around “Ichi-no-taira” on the summit, remains such as stone walls, dry moats, and the honmaru site survive; remains of “Ni-no-taira,” “San-no-kuruwa,” “Dan,” and northern and southern outworks are also recognized, and the area is designated as the Tottori Prefecture {{ill|lt=historic site|Q1081138|qid=Q1081138|1=Historic site}} “Futagamiyama Castle Ruins.” In academic surveys, over 100 fragments of Chinese/Korean celadon and pottery have been found around “Ni-no-taira,” and these features support that it functioned as a castle until the end of the Sengoku period<ref name="分県_二上山"/><ref name="新日本_二上山"/><ref name="TW_岩美_文化財"/><ref name="PREF-鳥_文化財_二上"/>.
=== Climbing === In the past, there was a byroad from Iwatsune village on the north side of the mountain over a pass called “'''Futagami-goe'''” to Kurami (former {{ill|lt=Fukube Village|Q5507614|qid=Q5507614|1=Fukube, Tottori}}) in the south<ref name="平凡_岩常"/>. Today, a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=regional farm road|1=UNKNOWN}} (Iwami Regional Farm Road) connects north and south, and near the pass is the Futagami-yama Tunnel. Along this farm road, a park has been developed with relocated Takanozaka kofun<ref name="分県_二上山"/><ref name="百名山_二上山"/>.
This park has a parking lot, and from there a hiking trail (Chūgoku Nature Trail) begins. There are two routes to the summit. The gentler one first goes south to Futagami-goe, then turns north toward the summit, reaching it in about one hour. On the other route, you proceed along the farm road for a while and then enter a steep stairway trail; this route reaches the summit via “Ni-no-taira” and “Dan”<ref name="分県_二上山"/><ref name="角川_二上山"/>.

}}
===Bibliography=== *'Expanded {{ill|lt=Dai Nihon Chimei Jisho|Q17213149|qid=Q17213149|1=Dai Nippon Chimei Jisho}}' Vol. 3 Chūgoku/Shikoku, by {{ill|lt=Yoshida Tōgo|Q11413910|qid=Q11413910|1=Tōgo Yoshida}}, {{ill|lt=Fuzanbō|Q11395102|qid=Q11395102|1=Fuzambo}}, 1900, 1970 *'Iwami Town History', Iwami Town History Publication Committee, 1968 *'Place Names of Tottori Prefecture ({{ill|lt=Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|Q17213509|qid=Q17213509|1=Japanese Historical Topography Series}})', {{ill|lt=Heibonsha|Q11482108|qid=Q11482108|1=Heibonsha}}, 1992 *'Nihon Chimei Daijiten 31: Tottori Prefecture ({{ill|lt=Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten|Q4093011|qid=Q4093011|1=Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten}})', {{ill|lt=Kadokawa Shoten|Q1054765|qid=Q1054765|1=Kadokawa Shoten}}, 1982, ISBN 978-4040013107 *'100 Famous Mountains of Tottori Prefecture', by Atsumi Nakajima, Ashi Shobō, 2002, ISBN 4-7512-0843-8 *'Shin Nihon Sangakushi', edited by Japanese Alpine Club, 2005, ISBN 978-4779500008 *'Shin Bunken Tozan Guide 30: Mountains of Tottori Prefecture', by Michihiro Fujiwara, Yama-to-Keikokusha, 2010, ISBN 978-4-635-02380-1
